title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe their practice of mindfulness or meditation. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Quiet Revolution: Four People on Mindfulness and Meditation

texts.A:
### Ana, 32, Yoga Teacher
[43]I discovered meditation at a difficult period in my life – a painful break-up followed by a period of unemployment that left me feeling anxious and purposeless.[/43] A friend persuaded me to attend a ten-day silent retreat, which I almost didn't go through with. The experience was transformative in ways I still find hard to articulate. [47]I became a yoga teacher partly as a result, because I wanted to help others access the stillness I had found and previously thought was unavailable to someone with a mind as busy as mine.[/47] [51]The most frequent misconception I encounter among new students is that meditation requires clearing the mind completely, which is a misunderstanding of the practice.[/51]
texts.B:
### Paul, 49, Journalist
[44]I was deeply sceptical of mindfulness for years, dismissing it as a wellness trend for people who hadn't tried therapy.[/44] What changed my mind was a piece I was assigned to write about stress in high-pressure professions. I interviewed several surgeons and emergency responders who practised meditation, and their accounts were so pragmatic and specific that I couldn't continue to dismiss it. [48]I started a simple five-minute breathing practice each morning mainly to give myself material for the article, and I've been doing it ever since.[/48] I wouldn't describe myself as a convert in any spiritual sense, but the practical benefits to my concentration and composure are real.
texts.C:
### Leila, 25, Pharmacist
[45]I started meditating during my final year of university exams when a tutor mentioned it as a technique for managing performance anxiety.[/45] I downloaded an app and followed guided sessions for about three months. The app helped initially, but I found after a while that the gamification elements – streaks, badges, reminders – were adding a new form of stress rather than reducing one. [49]I now practise without any technology, sitting quietly for twenty minutes each morning with no guidance or targets, which suits me far better.[/49] [52]I think the commercialisation of mindfulness is a double-edged sword – it has introduced millions of people to a genuinely valuable practice, but the way it's packaged can sometimes undermine its purpose.[/52]
texts.D:
### Derek, 61, Retired Manager
[46]My GP recommended mindfulness when she diagnosed me with high blood pressure and suggested I explore stress reduction techniques before resorting to medication.[/46] I was dubious but willing to try. I attended an eight-week mindfulness-based stress reduction course run by the local NHS trust, which was more rigorous and evidence-based than anything I'd imagined. [50]The scientific grounding of the course was what won me over – each session included research explaining why the techniques work, which appealed to the part of me that needs to understand the mechanism behind something before I trust it.[/50] My blood pressure is now within a normal range without medication, which I consider the best possible endorsement.

questions.question: began meditating during a particularly challenging period that involved both personal loss and professional uncertainty?
questions.explanation: Ana discovered meditation following a painful break-up and a period of unemployment that left her anxious and purposeless.

questions.question: had held a dismissive view of mindfulness before professional research caused them to reconsider?
questions.explanation: Paul dismissed mindfulness for years before being assigned an article about stress that involved interviewing people whose pragmatic accounts changed his mind.

questions.question: was introduced to meditation as a strategy for coping with the pressure of academic assessment?
questions.explanation: Leila started meditating during final university exams after a tutor mentioned it as a technique for managing performance anxiety.

questions.question: took up mindfulness on medical advice in preference to taking prescribed medicine?
questions.explanation: Derek's GP recommended stress reduction techniques before resorting to medication, after diagnosing him with high blood pressure.

questions.question: changed their career direction as a direct result of the benefits they gained from meditation?
questions.explanation: Ana became a yoga teacher partly because of her transformative experience at the retreat, wanting to help others access the stillness she had found.

questions.question: began a mindfulness practice initially for work-related reasons but found it personally beneficial?
questions.explanation: Paul started a five-minute breathing practice mainly to have material for his article, but has continued it ever since for the practical benefits.

questions.question: moved away from using a structured method because it was causing stress rather than reducing it?
questions.explanation: Leila stopped using the mindfulness app because the gamification elements were adding a new form of stress, and now practises without technology.

questions.question: was persuaded of the value of mindfulness by its scientific rather than spiritual credentials?
questions.explanation: Derek was won over by the scientific grounding of the course, which included research explaining why the techniques work.

questions.question: identifies a common wrong belief about what meditation actually involves?
questions.explanation: Ana says the most frequent misconception among new students is that meditation requires clearing the mind completely.

questions.question: offers a nuanced view about the widespread promotion of mindfulness in modern society?
questions.explanation: Leila says the commercialisation of mindfulness is a double-edged sword – it introduces people to a valuable practice but the packaging can undermine its purpose.
